turns.content: okay so i just looked at a calendar and i think im going to throw up
That sounds ominous. What did the calendar do to you?

my thesis final draft is due october 31. which is literally halloween. so i will be submitting a horror story about urban heat islands while other people are out getting drunk in costumes. genuinely terrified. this is my whole degree

October 31 gives you about four and a half months from now, which is genuinely a decent amount of time for a final draft -- but I understand why looking at it on a calendar makes it feel like it's tomorrow. When you say "final draft," what stage are you at currently? Do you have a draft in progress, or are you still in the data collection and analysis phase?

i have... a literature review that my supervisor said was "promising" which is academic speak for "this exists but please improve it." and i have some data from the preliminary heat mapping i did in april. but like. the actual analysis? the modelling? the discussion section? all of that is just vibes and anxiety at this point

Okay, so you have a literature review that needs strengthening and preliminary data, but the heavy analytical work hasn't started yet. That's actually not an unusual position for mid-year on a final-year thesis. Can I help you think through a rough timeline? Breaking the remaining work into chunks might make it feel less like one terrifying monolith.
yes please because my current strategy is "stare at the document and then close it and open tiktok" which is not working

Classic approach, very popular, zero results. Alright -- working backwards from October 31, you want a complete draft by mid-October for final editing and supervisor feedback. So roughly: June through July for finishing data collection and starting analysis. August for completing analysis, modelling, and drafting methods and results. September for the discussion, refining the lit review, and pulling it all together. Early October for full revision. Does that kind of breakdown make sense for the scope of what you're doing?

it does but august scares me because thats where all the hard stuff lives. like the modelling is the part i genuinely do not know how to do yet?? i keep pretending that future lily will somehow just figure it out but future lily is also me and i dont know things

Future you and present you have the same skill set, that's true. But present you has time to learn before August hits. What modelling are you using -- GIS-based heat mapping, statistical regression, something else? And does your supervisor have expertise in the specific method, or will you need to learn it more independently?

its gis based yeah. my supervisor knows gis but shes more ecology than spatial analysis so she can guide the interpretation but the actual technical stuff is on me. theres a postgrad in the lab who did similar modelling last year and i keep meaning to ask her for help but every time i see her i chicken out because she seems so busy and competent and im just here like a little fraud in a lab coat

Most postgrads are more willing to help than you'd expect -- especially if they did similar work recently, because they remember what it was like figuring it out. You don't need to ask for a huge time commitment. Something like "could I buy you a coffee and ask you about the GIS workflow you used for about twenty minutes" is low-pressure and specific. People respond well to bounded requests. The fraud feeling is extremely normal at this stage, by the way. It doesn't mean you're actually a fraud.

the coffee bribe... thats actually smart. i can do that. i literally make coffee for a living so i can definitely offer coffee as currency lol. okay so timeline plus actually asking for help from people who know things. wild concept. also set a friday check-in ritual. reward coffee after each check-in maybe. the barista in me demands ritual

Friday check-in followed by a reward coffee is a solid system -- pairing accountability with something you actually enjoy. Set yourself a small, specific goal each Friday: not "work on thesis" but something like "finish cleaning the April dataset" or "draft 500 words of methods." Concrete targets are easier to hit than vague ones. And if you get stuck on a section, switching to a different section is more productive than switching to TikTok.

switching sections instead of switching to tiktok... that is genuinely revolutionary advice for me. lowkey i needed someone to just tell me its doable and give me a plan because my brain has been treating this like one giant impossible task. four and a half months. i can do four and a half months. i think. probably. maybe

You can do four and a half months. You've already done the hardest part, which is choosing the topic and starting the work. The rest is showing up consistently. And for what it's worth, submitting a thesis on Halloween is objectively a great story for later.

no cap that is the one silver lining. submitting a thesis about how cities are literally cooking people alive on the spookiest day of the year?? thematic excellence. im going to submit it at 11:59pm in a witch hat for the narrative
That's the energy. Witch hat, 11:59 PM, urban heat island horror thesis. Document it for the group chat. Now go message that postgrad about coffee before you lose your nerve.
